overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-affected properties to the county board. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation - Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation - Conservation, Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation; staff explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone makes the existing structures conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve and forward to county board, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17th tornado affecting the Town of Ringle, Village of Kronenwetter, Town of Easton, and Village of Weston. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck questioned fairness to individual disaster victims not in declared disaster areas. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup costs. Eric Vogel asked whether homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Leur. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to limit the fee waiver to only reconnect fees for septic systems ($160), excluding all other zoning and septic fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed. The amended motion to forward to the county board passed, but not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher provided an overview of the department's operations, including the 600-acre landfill facility, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. Marilyn Bend praised the department for keeping private landfill costs in line.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department operates both city and county park systems with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees, manages 17 county parks and 30,700 acres of county forest. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, and Joe Tucker has been selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Two rezoning petitions forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for approval
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) forwarded to county board for consideration
Staff directed to track costs and requests related to any fee waivers implemented
Conservation Planning and Zoning to schedule summer educational meeting on conservation topics
title: Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
